chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content:
Seminar. (1-0). Credit 1. Investigation and report by students on topics of current
interest in computer science. Prerequisite: Junior or senior classification.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Junior or senior classification.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

*   Understand career opportunities and pathways, such as graduate school, for new
    graduates in computer science and computer engineering.
*   Apply techniques discussed when searching for jobs, interviewing, and negotiating.
*   Analyze ethical implications of professional decisions.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: None

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Optional
Reflective Writing
ISBN: 9781352010091
Authors: Kate Williams, Mary Woolliams, Jane Spiro
Publisher: Bloomsbury Publishing
Publication Date: 2020-05-09

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
The student's semester grade will be based on criteria explained below.

*   The final grade will be based on the number of assigned activities you complete successfully (as per
    given rubric or expectations), which can include (but not limited to) synchronous or asynchronous,
    recorded seminars; ethics assignments and assessments; other assigned activities at the instructor's
    discretion.

The grade is calculated as number of activities successfully completed, divided by the total number
of activities. Except for excused absences, these points cannot be made up, and extra credit will not
be offered. The grading scale is shown below.

chunks.title: Letter Grade to Point Scale Mapping
Grading Policy (continued)
chunks.content:
For example, if there are 14 activities, you can miss one and still get an A in the class,
two absences a B, and three absences and a C.

Only Canvas submissions are accepted for credit. It is your responsibility to make sure
that the submission process is completed and that the correct assignment is submitted
to the correct place. It is best to double check your submission by downloading it
yourself, then confirming that the one stored in Canvas is the intended one. Failure to
confirm correct submission is not a valid excuse for incomplete / late assignments

*   Guidelines for getting credit for synchronous seminars are described below, with complete
    instructions provided in the assignment. Non-compliance with the instructions will forfeit your
    credit for the seminar.

1.  Log into Zoom using SSO with your TAMU email, to allow us to match your
    login details with the official Howdy roster. Instructions for Zoom SSO login will
    be provided in Canvas.

2.  Make sure your zoom alias is: Last Name + COMMA + First Name, that match
    with Howdy.

chunks.title: Grading Policy (continued)
chunks.content:
3.  Always keep your video on / face visible. We will be taking random screenshots
    periodically during the talk.

4.  Answer polls in a timely manner, with each poll available for at least 1 minute.
    We will have a total of 5 polls and missing more than 2 (i.e., 3 or more) will
    forfeit your credit for the seminar.

5.  We have two separate channels of communication: Zoom for the actual seminar
    and questions to the speaker, and Discord for student interaction. The links can
    be accessed through Canvas. The reason for the separate channels is that
    questions to the speaker often get buried in the Zoom chat.

6.  You need to be online for at least 60 (cumulative) minutes according to the Zoom
    logs.
    Please arrive early or on time and refrain from leaving early (you may miss
    important announcements given towards the end of the class). If you experience
    technical difficulties, you can just re-enter the session at your earliest
    opportunity. It is your responsibility to make sure that you are in a place with
    good internet connection and you have an operational device, and a backup
    device when needed.

7.  You will submit a reflection with a minimum of 10 out of 18 points (~60%) to
    get credit (see sample rubric in the next item). Please refer to the corresponding
    reflection assignment and rubric for the specific scoring criteria which may be
    different from what is shown below.

8.  Late reflections, submitted on 11:59 p.m. (or after), the day after the seminar, will
    not be accepted for credit except for approved late submissions related to a
    Student Rule 7 absence. You can request approval for late submissions using the
    provided link in Canvas.

    Please use the same link to request for an excused absence at least 3 business
    days before your anticipated absence (except for emergencies). We will let you
    know if the request is approved or not.

*   With regards to the reflection, a template & rubric will be provided in each assignment. A sample
    reflection rubric with total points of 18 is shown below.

chunks.title: Reflection Rubric
chunks.content:
| Criteria | Ratings | Pts |
|:---------|:--------|:----|
| Content | 12 pts Meets Expectations Specific and personal (vs. generic); well-thought out. 8 pts Partially Meets Expectation Some specific, however there are some generic elements (e.g., seem like copy / paste) 4 pts Minimally Meets Expectations Mostly generic / general elements (e.g., seem like copy / paste) 0 pts No Marks Does not exist or Unacceptable (e.g., off topic) | 12 pts |
| Organization | 3 pts Meets Expectations Good organization; logically ordered ✓ aligned ideas; well-integrated; distinct beginning and end. 2 pts Partially Meets Expectation Organized; main ideas are somewhat order; sense of beginning and ending. 1 pts Minimally Meets Expectations Some organization; main ideas not complete / not good alignment; jump around; beginning and ending are unclear. 0 pts No Marks Poorly organized; no logical progression; beginning and ending are vague. | 3 pts |
| Syntax (grammar, usage, spelling) | 3 pts Meets Expectations No errors. 2 pts Partially Meets Expectation Few errors that do not distract. 1 pts Minimally Meets Expectations Enough errors that it is distracting. 0 pts No Marks Too many errors to count; document is unreadable. | 3 pts |
| Attendance via Zoom | (-18 pts) Did NOT Meet Zoom Attendance Requirement Not meeting attendance requirement, means that credit NOT given for this session. This item will result in a deduction of 18 points. 0 pts Met Zoom Attendance requirement. Good news! You met attendance requirements so your reflection will be graded according to the rubric | 0 pts |
| Word Count Met word count of 150 to 250 words | (-18 pts) Did NOT Meet Word Count Requirement Not meeting word count requirement, means that credit NOT given for this session. This item will result in a deduction of 18 points. (See note below table) 0 pts Met Word Count requirement. Good news! You met word count requirements so your reflection will be graded according to the rubric | 0 pts |

chunks.title: Reflection Rubric (continued)
chunks.content:
| Criteria | Ratings | Pts |
|:---------|:--------|:----|
| Plagiarism No plagiarism detected | (-18 pts) Exceeded Plagiarism Threshold Similarity score > 30% This item will result in a deduction of 18 points. 0 pts Met Plagiarism requirement. Good news! You met plagiarism requirement where similarity score < 30%. Your reflection will be graded according to the rubric | 0 pts |
| Highlightable Content | (-18 pts) Non-Highlightable Content This item will result in a deduction of 18 points. 0 pts Good news! You submitted highlightable content Your reflection will be graded according to the rubric | 0 pts |

chunks.title: Word Count Policy
chunks.content:
*   The word count is 150–220 words, with a 30-word buffer; 250 words is a hard limit, and no
    exceptions will be made, even at 251 words.

    Concise writing is a key professional skill because technical audiences need clear,
    focused communication that respects their limited time. It trains you to prioritize
    essential insights, state your main point clearly, and minimize filler, mirroring
    expectations for workplace emails and reports.

    The strict word limit also supports the course learning outcomes by requiring specific,
    meaningful reflections that demonstrate your ability to analyze each seminar, identify
    what matters most, and connect it to your own career and ethical decisions.

    Always verify your word count in Microsoft Word or in the Turnitin report (visible after
    you submit). Word counts from Canvas, Microsoft Word, and Turnitin are the only
    values that will be accepted.

*   Any evidence that the student did not solely write the reflection (Turnitin score greater than 30%)
    is considered academic misconduct and will be reported to the honors council and result in
    the application of an appropriate sanction. In addition, submission of a reflection when
    not in attendance (in the case of a synchronous seminar) can constitute academic
    misconduct.

*   You forfeit credit for the seminar when you do not meet the criteria previously
    outlined, especially in the following situations:

    *   You join the zoom meeting as a 'guest.'

chunks.title: Forfeiture Conditions and Excused Absences
chunks.content:
*   You don't attend at all
    Absences may only be excused as defined by the Texas A&M University
    Student Rules available at http://student-rules.tamu.edu/rule07. A student
    needs to submit documentation for the absence (e.g., doctor's note), using
    a link to be provided by the instructor, no later than 1 week after a
    student return to class, or the student will be automatically counted
    absent.

*   Offline for more than 15 minutes.
    If you have technical difficulties which leads to a loss of connection from
    the virtual meeting, just simply rejoin. However, if you foresee that this
    situation will occur frequently, you should seek a different location for future
    lectures. If you are online for at least 60 minutes, you will be counted present.

*   Face not visible / video off

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Late work is not accepted. Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused
absence (see Student Rule 7) is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work
policy. For excused absences, if the recording of the company talk is not available, then the
student will be given another makeup assignment by the instructor, which will either be offline
reading or watching another video.

Reasons for late submission requests not tied to absences (e.g., offline video submission) must
clearly be related to the criteria of excused absences according to Student Rule 7. The
instructor is under no obligation to accept late work if not related to an absence that is excused
under Student Rule 7.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy (continued)
chunks.content:
No late work will be accepted after the last day of class, irrespective of the deadline for an
assignment.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Class will be scheduled every Tuesday (no Thursday class), unless notified otherwise, with the
final schedule posted on Canvas.

chunks.title: Additional Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Expectations

Course expectations, in the form of a course appreciative agreement, will be reviewed and
agreed to at the beginning of the semester.

Course Copyright

The materials used within this course are copyrighted and include, but are not limited to, the
syllabus, course videos, audio, and visual recordings of classes, etc. Because these materials
are copyrighted, you do not have the right to copy or distribute these materials, unless permission
is expressly granted.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
Writing: Use of AI tools, including ChatGPT, is permitted in this course for students who wish to
use them. To adhere to our scholarly values, students must cite any AI-generated material that
informed their work (this includes in-text citations and/or use of quotations, and in your reference
list). Using an AI tool to generate content without proper attribution qualifies as academic
dishonesty.
See https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html fo
r information how to properly cite AI tools. (adapted from University of Massachusetts
Amherst, https://cte.tamu.edu/getmedia/1d5e4ef6-97f1-4065-987f-3c9dfecbb7bd/TAMU-
CTE GenAI-SyllabusStatementConsiderations.pdf)

Computer Code. In principle you may submit AI-generated code, or code that is based on or
derived from AI-generated code, as long as this use is properly documented in the comments:
you need to include the prompt and the significant parts of the response. AI tools may help you
avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that the generated code is correct. It is your
responsibility to identify errors in program logic through comprehensive, documented testing.
Moreover, generated code, even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for
improvement, in particular regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The
submission itself must meet our standards of attribution and validation.(from Boris Steipe (2023)
“Syllabus Resources”. The Sentient Syllabus Project http://sentientsyllabus.org.)
